---
title: "Building Containers on CoreHPC"
subtitle: "From conda environments to Apptainer images"
author: "Gladstone Bioinformatics Core"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    embed-resources: true
---

## Why build a container instead of running a conda environment?

On CoreHPC, the recommended way to use a long-lived software environment is to
capture it as a recipe, build it into a single **[Apptainer](https://apptainer.org/docs/user/main/)**
image (`.sif`), and run your code from that image instead of from a live conda
environment.

A conda environment is **hundreds of thousands of tiny files**. Stored on a
shared network filesystem like HIVE, those files are slow to read and slow to
scan. The cost is not just yours: large directories of small files degrade filesystem
performance for **everyone** on the cluster.

An Apptainer image solves this in three ways:

- **Reproducible.** The exact versions of every tool are pinned in the recipe
  and frozen into the image, which is a major benefit when you publish.
- **Tidy.** One `environment.yml` plus one `.sif` replaces a sprawling
  environment directory, so HIVE stays clean.
- **Fast.** The cluster reads **one file** instead of millions, which avoids the
  small-file penalty that conda environments impose on a network filesystem.

As a bonus, images are sharable making onboarding new lab members easier and allowing
you to easily share your environment (no more "but it works in my conda env").

::: {.callout-tip}
## Recommendation
Build each environment from a requirements file (`environment.yml`), bake it into
an Apptainer image, and delete the live conda environment once the image works.
Keep the recipe and the `.sif` on HIVE; rebuild from the recipe whenever you need
to.
:::

## The workflow at a glance

1. **Capture** your conda environment to an `environment.yml` recipe.
2. **Write** a short Apptainer definition file (`.def`).
3. **Build** the `.sif` image on the login node.
4. **Run** your code with `apptainer exec`.
5. **Store and clean up**: keep the recipe and image on HIVE, delete the live
   conda environment.

## Step 1: Capture your environment

From the login node, load Miniforge, activate the environment you want to
capture, and export it to a recipe:

```bash
module load miniforge3
conda activate myenv

# --no-builds drops platform-specific build strings, which keeps the
# recipe portable when the image is rebuilt.
conda env export --no-builds > environment.yml
```

The resulting `environment.yml` lists the Python version, every package, and the
exact versions. A trimmed example:

```yaml
name: myenv
channels:
  - conda-forge
dependencies:
  - python=3.11
  - numpy=1.26.4
  - pandas=2.2.2
  - scikit-learn=1.5.1
  - pip
  - pip:
      - some-pip-only-package==0.4.1
```

A couple of things to check before you build:

- **Channels:** make sure `conda-forge` is listed (CoreHPC's Miniforge uses it as
  the default channel). Remove `defaults` if it appears.
- **pip packages:** anything installed with `pip` shows up under a `pip:` block.
  Keep that block so the image picks those up too.
- You can delete the `prefix:` line at the bottom if present; it is ignored when
  building.

## Step 2: Write the Apptainer definition file

A definition file (`.def`) is the set of instructions Apptainer uses to build the
image. Create `myenv.def` next to your `environment.yml`:

```default
Bootstrap: docker
From: condaforge/miniforge3:latest

%files
    environment.yml /environment.yml

%post
    # Build the environment from the recipe, then trim the image.
    conda env create -f /environment.yml -n myenv
    conda clean -afy

%environment
    # Put the environment's tools on the PATH for every run.
    export PATH=/opt/conda/envs/myenv/bin:$PATH

%runscript
    exec "$@"
```

What each section does:

- **`Bootstrap` / `From`** start from the official Miniforge base image, so conda
  is already installed.
- **`%files`** copies your recipe into the image.
- **`%post`** runs at build time to create the environment and clean up cached
  package files.
- **`%environment`** runs every time you use the image; prepending the
  environment's `bin` to the `PATH` activates it without needing `conda activate`.
- **`%runscript`** makes `apptainer run` execute whatever command you pass.

::: {.callout-important}
The environment name appears twice: `-n myenv` in `%post` and
`/opt/conda/envs/myenv/bin` in `%environment`. Keep them identical, and match the
`name:` in your `environment.yml`.
:::

For the full set of definition-file sections and options, see the
[Apptainer definition file documentation](https://apptainer.org/docs/user/main/definition_files.html).

## Step 3: Build the image on the login node

```bash
module load apptainer
apptainer build myenv.sif myenv.def
```

The build pulls the base image and downloads your packages, so it must run where
there is internet. **The login node has proxied internet; the compute nodes have
none.** Build on the login node, then submit jobs that use the finished `.sif`.

## Step 4: Run your code

Execute any command inside the image with `apptainer exec`:

```bash
apptainer exec myenv.sif python script.py
```

For GPU code, add `--nv` so the container can see the GPU drivers (the same flag
you used on Wynton):

```bash
apptainer exec --nv myenv.sif python train.py
```

Inside a SLURM job, wrap it with `srun`:

```bash
srun apptainer exec --nv myenv.sif python train.py
```

By default Apptainer can see your home directory and the current working
directory. To make other paths (scratch, a HIVE share) available inside the
container, bind them explicitly:

```bash
apptainer exec --bind /mnt/scratch/user/$USER myenv.sif python script.py
```

You can also set `APPTAINER_BINDPATH` once instead of repeating `--bind`. See
[Bind paths and mounts](https://apptainer.org/docs/user/main/bind_paths_and_mounts.html)
for details.

## Step 5: Store the image and clean up

- Keep the recipe and image together on **HIVE**: `environment.yml`, `myenv.def`,
  and `myenv.sif`. The `.sif` is a single file, so it is friendly to the network
  filesystem.
- Once the image works, **delete the live conda environment** so it stops
  consuming space and slowing the filesystem:

  ```bash
  conda remove -n myenv --all
  ```

- You never lose anything by deleting the environment, rebuild it any time from
  the recipe with `apptainer build myenv.sif myenv.def`.

## More complex environments: build with Docker

Some environments need things conda cannot provide cleanly: system libraries,
`apt` packages, or tools you compile yourself. **R / Bioconductor** is the classic
example. Many Bioconductor packages are not on conda-forge, they are version-locked
to a specific Bioconductor release, and they will not compile until the right system
libraries are already in place. Reproducing that by hand is exactly the kind of
thorny setup containers exist to solve.

For these, build a **[Docker](https://docs.docker.com/)** image on your own machine.
The [Bioconductor](https://bioconductor.org/help/docker/) and
[rocker](https://rocker-project.org/) project base images already ship R, the
required system libraries, and a pinned Bioconductor release, so your Dockerfile
only has to add packages. Here is an example that builds a single-cell analysis
environment (Seurat plus harmony) on the Bioconductor base image:

```dockerfile
# Bioconductor base image gives access to many bioinformatics tools and R
# packages, with the R version and Bioconductor release already pinned.
FROM bioconductor/bioconductor_docker:RELEASE_3_17

# Exit if any command in a pipeline fails.
SHELL ["/bin/bash", "-o", "pipefail", "-c"]

# Update the package list and install system packages in one RUN, then clean up
# to keep the image small. (apt-get update and install must share a command.)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y \
    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends make \
    && apt-get clean \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Install the R packages, then confirm they load.
RUN Rscript -e 'install.packages(c("Seurat","harmony"))'
RUN Rscript -e 'lapply(c("Seurat","harmony"), library, character.only = TRUE)'

# Bake the Dockerfile into the image for reproducibility.
COPY Dockerfile /Dockerfile
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Why no non-root `USER`?
Adding a non-root `USER` near the end of a Dockerfile is a common Docker security
practice, but it is left out here on purpose. Apptainer runs the container as your
own cluster account and ignores the Dockerfile `USER`, so the step buys nothing on
CoreHPC. If you also run the image with Docker directly, add it back.
:::

Then build the image, push it to Docker Hub, and pull it onto CoreHPC as an
Apptainer image:

```bash
# On your laptop, in the directory with the Dockerfile. --platform linux/amd64
# matches CoreHPC's x86-64 CPUs; buildx builds and pushes in one step.
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 \
  -t docker_hub_user/seurat-harmony:1.0 --push .

# On the CoreHPC login node:
apptainer pull docker://docker_hub_user/seurat-harmony:1.0
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Building on Apple Silicon
M-series Macs build `linux/arm64` images by default, but CoreHPC runs x86-64. An
arm64 image fails on the cluster with `exec format error`, so always pass
`--platform linux/amd64`. That cross-build runs under emulation and is slow
(compiling Seurat takes a while and a lot of RAM), so build the image once and
reuse it rather than rebuilding for every job.
:::

`apptainer pull` converts the Docker image to a `seurat-harmony_1.0.sif` file you run
exactly like the one you built above (`apptainer exec seurat-harmony_1.0.sif Rscript analysis.R`).
Docker's own documentation covers writing
[Dockerfiles](https://docs.docker.com/reference/dockerfile/), choosing base
images, and [pushing to Docker Hub](https://docs.docker.com/get-started/introduction/build-and-push-first-image/).

## Quick reference

| Task                                | Command                                              |
|-------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
| Export a conda env to a recipe      | `conda env export --no-builds > environment.yml`     |
| Build an image from a `.def`        | `apptainer build myenv.sif myenv.def`                |
| Run a command in the image          | `apptainer exec myenv.sif python script.py`          |
| Run with GPU access                 | `apptainer exec --nv myenv.sif python train.py`      |
| Run inside a SLURM job              | `srun apptainer exec myenv.sif python script.py`     |
| Bind an extra path                  | `apptainer exec --bind /mnt/scratch/user/$USER ...`  |
| Convert a Docker image              | `apptainer pull docker://user/image:tag`             |
| Delete the live conda env           | `conda remove -n myenv --all`                        |
